questions.question_text: What is the expanded form of (x + 5)²?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Using (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b², where a = x and b = 5: x² + 2(x)(5) + 5² = x² + 10x + 25

Formula Reference: (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b²

questions.question_text: If (a - 3)² = a² - 6a + k, what is the value of k?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Using (a - b)² = a² - 2ab + b², where b = 3: Last term = b² = 3² = 9

Formula Reference: (a - b)² = a² - 2ab + b²

questions.question_text: What is (7 + 3)(7 - 3) equal to?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Using (a + b)(a - b) = a² - b²: 7² - 3² = 49 - 9 = 40

Formula Reference: (a + b)(a - b) = a² - b²

questions.question_text: Using the identity, calculate 98² mentally. (Hint: 98 = 100 - 2)
questions.subject: mathematics

questions.question_text: If x² + y² = 29 and xy = 10, what is (x + y)?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
(x + y)² = x² + 2xy + y² = 29 + 2(10) = 49. Therefore, x + y = 7 (taking positive root)

Formula Reference: (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b²

questions.question_text: Expand: (2x + 3)(2x - 3)
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Using a² - b² where a = 2x, b = 3: (2x)² - 3² = 4x² - 9

Formula Reference: (a + b)(a - b) = a² - b²

questions.question_text: If (x + 4)(x + 5) = x² + mx + 20, what is m?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Using the identity: middle term = (a + b)x = (4 + 5)x = 9x. So m = 9

Formula Reference: (x + a)(x + b) = x² + (a + b)x + ab

questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
x² - (2 + 7)x + (2)(7) = x² - 9x + 14

Formula Reference: (x - a)(x - b) = x² - (a + b)x + ab

questions.question_text: What is (a + b)³ - (a³ + b³) equal to?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
(a + b)³ = a³ + b³ + 3ab(a + b). So (a + b)³ - (a³ + b³) = 3ab(a + b)

Formula Reference: (a + b)³ = a³ + b³ + 3ab(a + b)

questions.question_text: If x + y = 10 and x² + y² = 58, what is xy?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
(x + y)² = x² + y² + 2xy. So 100 = 58 + 2xy. Therefore 2xy = 42, xy = 21

Formula Reference: (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b²

questions.question_text: Using identity, factorize: x² - 49
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
x² - 49 = x² - 7² = (x + 7)(x - 7)

Formula Reference: a² - b² = (a + b)(a - b)

questions.question_text: If a³ + b³ = 152 and a + b = 8, what is ab?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Also (a + b)² = a² + b² + 2ab, so 64 = a² + b² + 2ab. From a³ + b³ = (a + b)(a² + b² - ab): 152 = 8(a² + b² - ab). From both equations, ab = 15

Formula Reference: a³ + b³ = (a + b)(a² - ab + b²)

questions.question_text: Simplify: (x + 2)² - (x - 2)²
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
(x² + 4x + 4) - (x² - 4x + 4) = x² + 4x + 4 - x² + 4x - 4 = 8x

Formula Reference: (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b², (a - b)² = a² - 2ab + b²

questions.question_text: What is the total surface area of a cube with side 5 cm?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Surface area = 6a² = 6 × 5² = 6 × 25 = 150 cm²

Formula Reference: Surface area of cube = 6a²

questions.question_text: A cuboid has dimensions: length = 8 cm, breadth = 5 cm, height = 3 cm. What is its volume?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Volume = l × b × h = 8 × 5 × 3 = 120 cm³

Formula Reference: Volume of cuboid = l × b × h

questions.question_text: The radius of a cylinder is 7 cm and height is 10 cm. What is its volume? (Use π = 22/7)
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Volume = πr²h = (22/7) × 7² × 10 = (22/7) × 49 × 10 = 1540 cm³

Formula Reference: Volume of cylinder = πr²h

questions.question_text: If the side of a cube is doubled, its volume becomes:
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
New volume = (2a)³ = 8a³. So volume becomes 8 times the original

Formula Reference: Volume of cube = a³

questions.question_text: A sphere has radius 3 cm. What is its surface area? (Use π = 3.14)
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Surface area = 4πr² = 4 × 3.14 × 3² = 4 × 3.14 × 9 = 113.04 cm²

Formula Reference: Surface area of sphere = 4πr²

questions.question_text: A cone has radius 6 cm and height 8 cm. What is its slant height?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
l² = h² + r² = 8² + 6² = 64 + 36 = 100. So l = 10 cm

Formula Reference: l² = h² + r² (slant height formula)

questions.question_text: If the radius of a sphere is halved, its volume becomes:
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
New volume = (4/3)π(r/2)³ = (4/3)πr³/8 = Volume/8. So it becomes 1/8th

Formula Reference: Volume of sphere = (4/3)πr³

questions.question_text: The volume of a cube is 125 cm³. What is the length of its side?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
a³ = 125, so a = ∛125 = 5 cm

Formula Reference: Volume of cube = a³

questions.question_text: A cylinder and a cone have the same radius and height. The volume of cylinder is how many times the volume of cone?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Cylinder volume = πr²h, Cone volume = (1/3)πr²h. Ratio = πr²h ÷ (1/3)πr²h = 3

Formula Reference: Cylinder volume = πr²h, Cone volume = (1/3)πr²h

questions.question_text: The curved surface area of a cylinder with radius 5 cm and height 10 cm is: (Use π = 3.14)
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Curved surface area = 2πrh = 2 × 3.14 × 5 × 10 = 314 cm²

Formula Reference: Total surface area = 2πr(h + r), Curved surface = 2πrh

questions.question_text: A triangle has sides 3 cm, 4 cm, and 5 cm. What is its semi-perimeter?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Semi-perimeter s = (3 + 4 + 5)/2 = 12/2 = 6 cm

Formula Reference: s = (a + b + c)/2

questions.question_text: Find the mean of: 5, 8, 12, 15, 20
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Mean = (5 + 8 + 12 + 15 + 20)/5 = 60/5 = 12

Formula Reference: Mean = Sum of observations / Number of observations

questions.question_text: Find the median of: 3, 7, 9, 12, 15
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
n = 5 (odd). Median = [(5+1)/2]th term = 3rd term = 9

Formula Reference: Median (odd n) = [(n+1)/2]th term

questions.question_text: Find the median of: 4, 8, 10, 12, 15, 18
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
n = 6 (even). Median = Mean of 3rd and 4th terms = (10 + 12)/2 = 11

Formula Reference: Median (even n) = Mean of (n/2)th and (n/2 + 1)th terms

questions.question_text: In the data: 5, 7, 7, 9, 11, 7, 13, what is the mode?

questions.answer_explanation:

questions.question_text: The mean of 5 numbers is 20. If one number is 25, what is the sum of the other 4 numbers?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Total sum = Mean × n = 20 × 5 = 100. Sum of other 4 = 100 - 25 = 75

Formula Reference: Mean = Sum / n, so Sum = Mean × n

questions.question_text: The mean of 6 observations is 15. If one more observation of 27 is added, what is the new mean?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
Old sum = 15 × 6 = 90. New sum = 90 + 27 = 117. New mean = 117/7 ≈ 16.71

Formula Reference: Mean = Sum of observations / Number of observations

questions.question_text: If all observations in a dataset are increased by 5, what happens to the mean?
questions.subject: mathematics
questions.answer_explanation:
New sum = Old sum + 5n. New mean = (Old sum + 5n)/n = Old mean + 5. Mean increases by 5.

Formula Reference: Mean = Sum / n

questions.question_text: What would happen if the plasma membrane ruptures or breaks down?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: If the plasma membrane ruptures, substances will freely move in/out uncontrolled. The cell loses selective permeability, protoplasm leaks out, and the cell dies.

questions.question_text: Why doesn't water gather in the boiled potato cup in the osmosis experiment?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Boiling kills potato cells, destroying the selectively permeable membrane. Without a functional membrane, osmosis cannot occur.

questions.question_text: Who discovered cells and in which year?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Robert Hooke discovered cells in 1665 while examining a thin slice of cork under a microscope. He named them 'cells' because they resembled honeycomb compartments.

questions.question_text: What material was Robert Hooke observing when he discovered cells?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Robert Hooke was examining a thin slice of cork (dead plant tissue from tree bark) when he discovered cells that looked like honeycomb compartments.

questions.question_text: Why is the cell called the structural and functional unit of life?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Cells form body structure (tissues → organs → organ systems) and perform all life processes (respiration, digestion, excretion) independently in both unicellular and multicellular organisms.

questions.question_text: How does CO₂ move out of the cell?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: CO₂ moves by diffusion from high concentration (inside cell where it's produced as waste) to low concentration (outside cell) across the cell membrane.

questions.question_text: How does water move in and out of the cell?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Water moves by osmosis - diffusion of water through a selectively permeable membrane from high to low concentration until equilibrium is reached.

questions.question_text: Why is the plasma membrane called a selectively permeable membrane?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: The plasma membrane allows only certain molecules to pass through while blocking others. It controls what enters and exits the cell, not allowing free movement of all substances.

questions.question_text: What is the typical size range of prokaryotic cells?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Prokaryotic cells are generally small, ranging from 1-10 μm (micrometers). They're smaller than eukaryotic cells (5-100 μm).

questions.question_text: What is the poorly defined nuclear region in prokaryotic cells called?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: The nucleoid is the region in prokaryotic cells where genetic material is located. It lacks a nuclear membrane, making it poorly defined.

questions.question_text: Do prokaryotic cells have membrane-bound organelles?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: False - Prokaryotic cells lack membrane-bound organelles like mitochondria, ER, Golgi apparatus. Only eukaryotic cells have these.

questions.question_text: How many chromosomes do prokaryotic cells typically have?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Prokaryotic cells have a single circular chromosome. Eukaryotic cells have multiple linear chromosomes.

questions.question_text: What is the typical size range of eukaryotic cells?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Eukaryotic cells are generally large, ranging from 5-100 μm. They're larger than prokaryotic cells (1-10 μm).

questions.question_text: Name the two organelles that contain their own genetic material (DNA).
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Mitochondria and plastids (chloroplasts) have their own DNA and ribosomes. This supports the endosymbiotic theory that they were once independent organisms.

questions.question_text: Why are lysosomes called 'suicide bags' of the cell?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: When a cell is damaged beyond repair, lysosomes burst and release digestive enzymes that digest the cell's own components, causing cell death.

questions.question_text: Where are proteins synthesized inside the cell?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Ribosomes synthesize proteins, earning them the name 'protein factories'. They're found free in cytoplasm or attached to rough ER.

questions.question_text: Do plant cells have a cell wall?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: True - Plant cells have a rigid cell wall made of cellulose outside the plasma membrane. Animal cells lack a cell wall.

questions.question_text: What is the typical shape of plant cells?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Plant cells are rectangular or square-shaped with distinct edges due to the rigid cell wall. Animal cells are round and irregular.

questions.question_text: Where is the nucleus located in plant cells?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: In plant cells, the nucleus is pushed to one side by the large central vacuole. In animal cells, it's centrally located.

questions.question_text: Are plastids present in animal cells?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: False - Plastids (including chloroplasts) are present only in plant cells. They help in photosynthesis and storage.

questions.question_text: How many vacuoles do plant cells typically have?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Plant cells typically have one or a few large, centrally located vacuoles. Animal cells have many small vacuoles.

questions.question_text: What would happen if there was no Golgi apparatus in a cell?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Without Golgi apparatus, cells cannot package, modify, or dispatch materials. Cell wall formation would also be affected. The cell cannot function properly.

questions.question_text: Which organelle is known as the 'powerhouse of the cell'?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Mitochondria are the powerhouse because they produce ATP (energy currency) through cellular respiration, providing energy for all cell activities.

questions.question_text: What is ATP often called?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: ATP (Adenosine triphosphate) is called the 'energy currency' because it stores and transfers energy for cellular activities, like money in an economy.

questions.question_text: Where are lipids and proteins of the cell membrane synthesized?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: The Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER) synthesizes lipids and proteins. Rough ER makes proteins, smooth ER makes lipids.

questions.question_text: How does Amoeba obtain its food?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Amoeba uses endocytosis - it engulfs food particles with pseudopodia (false feet), forming a food vacuole. Enzymes then digest the food inside.

questions.question_text: What is osmosis?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water molecules through a selectively permeable membrane from high to low water concentration until equilibrium is reached.

questions.question_text: In the potato osmosis experiment, why does water gather in potato cups containing sugar or salt?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Water concentration is higher outside (pure water) than inside the potato cup (sugar/salt solution). Water moves in by osmosis through the potato cells acting as semipermeable membrane.

questions.question_text: Which type of cell division is required for growth and repair of the body?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Mitosis produces identical daughter cells for growth and repair. Meiosis produces gametes (sex cells) with half the chromosomes.

questions.question_text: Which type of cell division is involved in the formation of gametes (sex cells)?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Meiosis produces gametes with half the chromosome number. This ensures offspring get the correct chromosome number when gametes fuse.

questions.question_text: What is a tissue?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: A tissue is a group of similar cells working together to perform a specific function. Examples: muscle tissue for movement, nervous tissue for signaling.

questions.question_text: What is the utility of tissues in multicellular organisms?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Tissues provide structural strength and enable division of labor - different tissues specialize in different functions, making the organism more efficient.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a type of simple tissue in plants?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Simple tissues include parenchyma, collenchyma, and sclerenchyma. Xylem is a complex tissue made of multiple cell types.

questions.question_text: Where is apical meristem found in plants?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Apical meristem is found at the tips of roots and shoots, responsible for increase in length (primary growth) of the plant.

questions.question_text: Which tissue makes up the husk of a coconut?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Sclerenchyma tissue has thick, lignified cell walls and dead cells, making it hard and stiff - perfect for the protective coconut husk.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a constituent of phloem?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Phloem consists of sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem parenchyma, and phloem fibers. Vessels are part of xylem, not phloem.

questions.question_text: Which two tissues are jointly responsible for movement in our body?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Muscular tissue contracts to produce movement, while nervous tissue sends signals to control and coordinate these movements.

questions.question_text: What is the long, thin extension of a neuron that carries signals away from the cell body called?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: The axon is the long fiber that transmits electrical impulses away from the neuron's cell body to other neurons or muscles. It can be over a meter long.

questions.question_text: What are the short, branched structures of a neuron that receive signals called?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Dendrites are short, branched extensions that receive signals from other neurons and transmit them toward the cell body.

questions.question_text: Are cardiac muscles voluntary or involuntary?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Cardiac muscles are involuntary - they contract automatically without conscious control, continuously pumping blood throughout life.

questions.question_text: What is the shape of cardiac muscle cells?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Cardiac muscle cells are cylindrical and branched, with striations. They're uninucleated (one nucleus per cell).

questions.question_text: Are cardiac muscle fibers striated (having bands)?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: True - Cardiac muscles have striations (light and dark bands) due to the arrangement of protein filaments, similar to skeletal muscles.

questions.question_text: What is the function of areolar tissue?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Areolar tissue is loose connective tissue found between skin and muscles, around blood vessels and nerves. It supports organs, fills spaces, and helps in tissue repair.

questions.question_text: How many types of elements make up xylem tissue?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Xylem has 4 elements: vessels, tracheids, xylem fibers, and xylem parenchyma. They work together to transport water and minerals.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT an element of xylem?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Xylem consists of vessels, tracheids, xylem fibers, and xylem parenchyma. Companion cells are part of phloem, not xylem.

questions.question_text: What is the main difference between simple and complex tissues in plants?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Simple tissues have one type of cell (e.g., parenchyma). Complex tissues have multiple cell types working together (e.g., xylem with vessels, tracheids, fibers).

questions.question_text: Which type of plant tissue has cell walls thickened with lignin?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Sclerenchyma has thick cell walls due to lignin deposition, making it hard and providing mechanical support. Cells are dead at maturity.

questions.question_text: Which plant tissue has cell walls thickened at the corners with pectin?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Collenchyma has cell walls thickened at corners with pectin, providing flexible mechanical support to young stems and leaves. Cells are living.

questions.question_text: What are the tiny pores on the surface of leaves called?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Stomata are pores in the epidermis that allow gas exchange (CO₂ in, O₂ out) and transpiration (water vapor loss).

questions.question_text: What are the functions of stomata?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Stomata perform two main functions: gas exchange (CO₂/O₂) for photosynthesis and respiration, and transpiration (water loss) for cooling and nutrient transport.

questions.question_text: Where are striated muscles found in the body?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Striated (skeletal) muscles are attached to bones and control voluntary movements like walking, writing, lifting. They're long, cylindrical, and multinucleated.

questions.question_text: Are striated muscles voluntary or involuntary?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Striated (skeletal) muscles are voluntary - we consciously control them for movements like walking, talking, writing.

questions.question_text: Where are smooth (unstriated) muscles found?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Smooth muscles are in walls of internal organs like stomach, intestines, blood vessels, and airways. They're involuntary, spindle-shaped, and uninucleated.

questions.question_text: Which is a lustrous non-metal?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Iodine is a non-metal that shows metallic luster. It appears as shiny, grayish-black crystals.

questions.question_text: What is the specific function of cardiac muscles?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Cardiac muscles contract and relax rhythmically throughout life to pump blood continuously. They work without fatigue or conscious control.

questions.question_text: Which tissue forms the inner lining of the mouth?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Squamous epithelium (flat, tile-like cells) lines the mouth, providing a smooth surface and protection. It's also found in blood vessels and alveoli.

questions.question_text: Which tissue connects muscle to bone in humans?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Tendons are tough, fibrous connective tissue cords that attach muscles to bones, enabling movement when muscles contract.

questions.question_text: Which tissue transports food in plants?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Phloem transports prepared food (sugars) from leaves to all parts of the plant. Xylem transports water and minerals.

questions.question_text: Which tissue stores fat in our body?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Adipose tissue is a type of connective tissue that stores fat, provides insulation, and acts as an energy reserve.

questions.question_text: Which connective tissue has a fluid matrix?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Blood is a fluid connective tissue with plasma (liquid matrix) containing blood cells. It transports oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and waste.

questions.question_text: Which tissue is present in the brain?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Nervous tissue makes up the brain, spinal cord, and nerves. It receives stimuli, processes information, and transmits signals.

questions.question_text: What type of tissue is found in the bark of a tree?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Bark consists of cork tissue (protective tissue) with dead cells, thick cell walls with suberin deposition, providing protection from injury and water loss.

questions.question_text: What type of epithelial tissue lines the kidney tubules?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Cuboidal epithelium (cube-shaped cells) lines kidney tubules, providing mechanical support and involved in secretion and absorption.

questions.question_text: Where is parenchyma tissue found in plants?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Parenchyma is found in stem/root pith, leaves (as chlorenchyma with chlorophyll), and aquatic plants (as aerenchyma with air cavities for floating).

questions.question_text: What is the role of epidermis in plants?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Epidermis is the outer protective layer of plants with no intercellular spaces. It prevents water loss, protects against injury, and regulates gas exchange.

questions.question_text: How does cork act as a protective tissue?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Cork has dead cells arranged densely with no intercellular spaces. Cell walls have suberin deposition, making them impervious to water and gases, providing excellent protection.

questions.question_text: Which separation technique should be used to separate mercury and water?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Decantation is used because mercury is denser than water and doesn't mix with it. Mercury settles at the bottom and water can be poured off carefully.

questions.question_text: Which separation technique should be used to separate potassium chloride and ammonium chloride?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Sublimation works because ammonium chloride changes directly from solid to gas on heating, while potassium chloride doesn't sublime.

questions.question_text: To separate a mixture of common salt, water, and sand, which techniques should be used?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: First, filtration removes sand (insoluble solid). Then evaporation removes water, leaving salt crystals behind.

questions.question_text: Which technique can be used to separate salt from its solution, besides evaporation?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Crystallization involves heating the solution to form a saturated solution, then cooling it to form pure crystals of the solute.

questions.question_text: Which technique should be used to recover acetone (boiling point 56°C) from a salt-water solution?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Distillation separates liquids based on boiling points. Acetone is volatile (low BP of 56°C) so it evaporates and condenses first, separating from water and salt.

questions.question_text: What happens when a saturated solution of potassium chloride prepared at 60°C is cooled to room temperature?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: When temperature decreases, solubility decreases. The solution becomes supersaturated and excess solute crystallizes out as solid crystals.

questions.question_text: What happens when an aqueous sugar solution is heated to dryness?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Water evaporates first. Continued heating causes sugar to decompose and turn black (charred) due to breakdown of sugar molecules.

questions.question_text: What happens when a mixture of iron filings and sulfur powder is heated strongly?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: A chemical reaction occurs: Fe + S → FeS. Ferrous sulfide is formed with completely different properties from iron and sulfur.

questions.question_text: Why do colloidal particles not settle down when left undisturbed?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Colloidal particles are very small (1-100 nm) and exhibit Brownian motion - random zig-zag movement that counteracts gravity, preventing settling.

questions.question_text: Smoke and fog are both aerosols. What is the main difference between them?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Both have air as the dispersion medium. Smoke has solid carbon particles dispersed in air, while fog has liquid water droplets dispersed in air.

questions.question_text: Is the composition of steel (98% iron, 1.5% carbon, 0.5% other elements) a physical or chemical property?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Physical property - steel is an alloy (homogeneous mixture), not a compound. No new chemical substance is formed; elements are just mixed.

questions.question_text: Is zinc dissolving in hydrochloric acid with evolution of hydrogen gas a physical or chemical property?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Chemical property - a chemical reaction occurs: Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂. New substances (zinc chloride and hydrogen) are formed.

questions.question_text: Is metallic sodium being soft enough to cut with a knife a physical or chemical property?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Physical property - cutting changes only the shape/size, not the chemical composition. No new substance is formed.

questions.question_text: Which student correctly prepared a 50% (mass by volume) NaOH solution: A dissolved 50g NaOH in 100mL water; B dissolved 50g in 100g water; C dissolved 50g in water to make 100mL solution?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Student C is correct. 50% (m/v) means 50g solute per 100mL total solution. A and B made 150mL solution. C made exactly 100mL final solution.

questions.question_text: What process is associated with dry ice kept at room temperature and atmospheric pressure?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Sublimation - dry ice (solid CO₂) changes directly from solid to gas without becoming liquid at normal atmospheric pressure.

questions.question_text: What process is associated with a drop of ink spreading throughout water?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Diffusion - particles of ink move randomly and spread from high concentration to low concentration, mixing uniformly with water.

questions.question_text: What process is associated with an open acetone bottle becoming empty over time?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Evaporation - acetone molecules at the surface gain enough energy to escape into air as vapor, gradually emptying the bottle.

questions.question_text: What process is used when milk is churned to separate cream?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Centrifugation - rapid spinning creates centrifugal force that separates components based on density. Lighter cream separates from denser milk.

questions.question_text: What process causes sand to settle when a sand-water mixture is left undisturbed?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Sedimentation - heavier sand particles settle down at the bottom due to gravity when the mixture is left undisturbed.

questions.question_text: What phenomenon occurs when a fine beam of light in a dark room illuminates particles in its path?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Tyndall effect - light is scattered by colloidal particles in its path, making the beam visible. This helps identify colloidal solutions.

questions.question_text: Sample A of water boils at 100°C, and sample B boils at 102°C. Which sample will NOT freeze at 0°C?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Sample B won't freeze at 0°C. Impurities raise boiling point and lower freezing point. Since B boils at 102°C (higher), it has impurities and will freeze below 0°C.

questions.question_text: Why is pure gold alloyed with copper or silver to make ornaments?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Pure gold is very soft and bends easily. Alloying with copper/silver adds strength and hardness, making ornaments more durable.

questions.question_text: An element is sonorous and highly ductile. Under which category would you classify it?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Metals are sonorous (produce sound when struck) and ductile (can be drawn into wires). These are characteristic properties of metals.

questions.question_text: A mixture of acetone and water can be separated by:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Distillation separates miscible liquids with different boiling points. Acetone (BP 56°C) is volatile and evaporates before water (BP 100°C).

questions.question_text: A mixture of oil and water can be separated using a:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Separating funnel works for immiscible liquids with different densities. Oil floats on water and can be separated through the funnel.

questions.question_text: Chromatography is used to separate:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Chromatography separates colored components dissolved in a solvent based on their different rates of movement through a medium (like paper).

questions.question_text: A colloid is a _____ mixture.
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Colloids are heterogeneous mixtures where particles (1-100 nm) are dispersed but not dissolved. They appear homogeneous but are actually heterogeneous.

questions.question_text: What technique can separate components of a colloidal solution?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Centrifugation uses high-speed rotation to separate colloidal particles from the dispersion medium based on density differences.

questions.question_text: Ice, water, and water vapor are _____ the same.
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: All three are H₂O molecules - chemically identical. They differ only in physical state (solid, liquid, gas) and physical properties.

questions.question_text: In a chloroform-water mixture in a separating funnel, which liquid forms the upper layer?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Water is less dense than chloroform, so it floats on top. Chloroform (density ~1.5 g/cm³) settles at the bottom.

questions.question_text: Two miscible liquids with boiling points differing by less than 25K can be separated by:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Fractional distillation uses a fractionating column with multiple condensation-evaporation cycles to separate liquids with close boiling points.

questions.question_text: When light passes through milk diluted in water, it shows a bluish tinge. This phenomenon is called:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Tyndall effect - colloidal particles in milk scatter light, making the beam visible and giving it a bluish color.

questions.question_text: Sugar crystals from sugarcane and beetroot mixed together form a:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Pure substance - both are chemically identical (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁). The source doesn't matter; the chemical composition is the same.

questions.question_text: Can we separate alcohol dissolved in water using a separating funnel?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: False - alcohol and water are miscible (mix completely). Separating funnel only works for immiscible liquids. Use distillation instead.

questions.question_text: Is heating calcium carbonate to form calcium oxide and carbon dioxide a physical or chemical change?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Chemical change - new substances (CaO and CO₂) with different properties are formed. Reaction: CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂

questions.question_text: Which non-metal exists as a liquid at room temperature?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Bromine is the only non-metal that is liquid at room temperature. It's a reddish-brown volatile liquid.

questions.question_text: Which allotrope of carbon is a good conductor of electricity?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Graphite has free electrons in its layered structure that can move freely, making it a good conductor of electricity.

questions.question_text: Which non-metal is known to form the largest number of compounds?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Carbon forms millions of compounds due to its ability to form four covalent bonds and catenate (form chains with itself).

questions.question_text: Which non-metal is required for combustion (burning)?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Oxygen supports combustion. It's a reactant in burning reactions, combining with fuel to release energy.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is NOT a compound?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Iron is an element (pure substance containing only one type of atom). Compounds contain two or more elements chemically combined.

questions.question_text: In a reaction, 5.3g sodium carbonate reacted with 6g acetic acid to produce 2.2g CO₂, 0.9g water, and 8.2g sodium acetate. What is the total mass of reactants?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Total mass of reactants = 5.3g + 6g = 11.3g. This equals the total mass of products (2.2g + 0.9g + 8.2g = 11.3g), proving the law of conservation of mass.

questions.question_text: Does the reaction (5.3g sodium carbonate + 6g acetic acid → 2.2g CO₂ + 0.9g water + 8.2g sodium acetate) follow the law of conservation of mass?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: True - Reactants: 5.3g + 6g = 11.3g. Products: 2.2g + 0.9g + 8.2g = 11.3g. Mass is conserved, proving the law.

questions.question_text: Hydrogen and oxygen combine in the ratio 1:8 by mass to form water. What mass of oxygen is needed to react with 3g of hydrogen?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: The ratio is 1:8 (H:O). For 3g hydrogen: 3 × 8 = 24g oxygen. This demonstrates the law of definite proportions.

questions.question_text: Which postulate of Dalton's atomic theory is the result of the law of conservation of mass?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: The postulate 'Atoms cannot be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction' explains why mass is conserved - atoms just rearrange, total number stays the same.

questions.question_text: Which postulate of Dalton's atomic theory explains the law of definite proportions?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: The postulate that 'relative number and kinds of atoms are constant in a given compound' explains why compounds always have the same composition by mass.

questions.question_text: What is the molar mass of ethyne (C₂H₂)? (Atomic masses: C = 12u, H = 1u)
questions.subject: science

questions.question_text: One atomic mass unit (amu) is equal to what fraction of the mass of one carbon-12 atom?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: By definition, 1 amu = 1/12th the mass of one carbon-12 atom. This provides a standard reference for atomic masses.

questions.question_text: Why can't we see atoms with naked eyes?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Atoms are extremely small (measured in nanometers, 10⁻⁹ m) and most don't exist independently. They're far below the resolution limit of human eyes.

questions.question_text: What is the chemical formula of sodium oxide?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Na₂O - Sodium has valency +1, oxygen has valency -2. To balance: 2 Na atoms + 1 O atom = Na₂O.

questions.question_text: What is the chemical formula of aluminium chloride?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: AlCl₃ - Aluminium has valency +3, chlorine has valency -1. To balance: 1 Al atom + 3 Cl atoms = AlCl₃.

questions.question_text: What is the chemical formula of magnesium hydroxide?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Mg(OH)₂ - Magnesium has valency +2, hydroxide (OH) has valency -1. To balance: 1 Mg + 2 OH groups = Mg(OH)₂.

questions.question_text: What is the name of the compound Al₂(SO₄)₃?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Aluminium sulphate - Al₂(SO₄)₃ contains 2 aluminium atoms and 3 sulfate (SO₄²⁻) groups.

questions.question_text: What is the name of the compound CaCl₂?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Calcium chloride - CaCl₂ contains 1 calcium atom and 2 chlorine atoms.

questions.question_text: What is the name of the compound K₂SO₄?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Potassium sulphate - K₂SO₄ contains 2 potassium atoms and 1 sulfate (SO₄²⁻) group.

questions.question_text: What is the name of the compound KNO₃?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Potassium nitrate - KNO₃ contains 1 potassium atom and 1 nitrate (NO₃⁻) group.

questions.question_text: What is the name of the compound CaCO₃?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Calcium carbonate - CaCO₃ contains 1 calcium atom and 1 carbonate (CO₃²⁻) group. Found in limestone and marble.

questions.question_text: How many total atoms are present in one H₂S molecule?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 3 atoms total - H₂S has 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 sulfur atom.

questions.question_text: How many total atoms are present in one PO₄³⁻ ion?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 5 atoms total - PO₄³⁻ has 1 phosphorus atom and 4 oxygen atoms.

questions.question_text: What is the molecular mass of H₂? (Atomic mass of H = 1u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 2u - Molecular mass = 2 × atomic mass of H = 2 × 1u = 2u

questions.question_text: What is the molecular mass of O₂? (Atomic mass of O = 16u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 32u - Molecular mass = 2 × atomic mass of O = 2 × 16u = 32u

questions.question_text: What is the molecular mass of CO₂? (Atomic masses: C = 12u, O = 16u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 44u - Molecular mass = 12u + (2 × 16u) = 12u + 32u = 44u

questions.question_text: What is the molecular mass of CH₄? (Atomic masses: C = 12u, H = 1u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 16u - Molecular mass = 12u + (4 × 1u) = 12u + 4u = 16u

questions.question_text: What is the molecular mass of NH₃? (Atomic masses: N = 14u, H = 1u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 17u - Molecular mass = 14u + (3 × 1u) = 14u + 3u = 17u

questions.question_text: What is the formula unit mass of ZnO? (Atomic masses: Zn = 65u, O = 16u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 81u - Formula unit mass = 65u + 16u = 81u

questions.question_text: What is the formula unit mass of Na₂O? (Atomic masses: Na = 23u, O = 16u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 62u - Formula unit mass = (2 × 23u) + 16u = 46u + 16u = 62u

questions.question_text: What is the formula unit mass of K₂CO₃? (Atomic masses: K = 39u, C = 12u, O = 16u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 138u - Formula unit mass = (2 × 39u) + 12u + (3 × 16u) = 78u + 12u + 48u = 138u

questions.question_text: If 1 mole of carbon atoms weighs 12g, what is the mass of 1 carbon atom?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 1.99 × 10⁻²³ g - Mass of 1 atom = 12g ÷ (6.022 × 10²³) = 1.99 × 10⁻²³ g. This shows how incredibly small individual atoms are.

questions.question_text: Which has more atoms: 100g of sodium (Na = 23u) or 100g of iron (Fe = 56u)?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 100g sodium has more atoms. Na: (100÷23) × 6.022×10²³ = 26.18×10²³ atoms. Fe: (100÷56) × 6.022×10²³ = 10.75×10²³ atoms. Lighter atoms mean more atoms in same mass.

questions.question_text: A compound of boron and oxygen has 0.096g boron and 0.144g oxygen (total 0.24g). What is the percentage of boron?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 40% - Percentage of boron = (0.096 ÷ 0.24) × 100 = 40%. This means oxygen is 60%.

questions.question_text: When 3g carbon burns in 8g oxygen, 11g CO₂ forms. If 3g carbon burns in 50g oxygen, how much CO₂ forms?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 11g CO₂ - By law of definite proportions, 3g carbon always needs exactly 8g oxygen. Excess 42g oxygen remains unreacted. Same product (11g) forms.

questions.question_text: What are polyatomic ions?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Polyatomic ions are groups of atoms carrying a charge that behave as a single unit. Examples: CO₃²⁻ (carbonate), SO₄²⁻ (sulfate), NH₄⁺ (ammonium).

questions.question_text: What is the chemical formula of calcium carbonate?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: CaCO₃ - Calcium (Ca²⁺) combines with carbonate (CO₃²⁻) in 1:1 ratio.

questions.question_text: Which elements are present in quick lime (CaO)?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Quick lime is CaO, containing calcium (Ca) and oxygen (O). Used in construction and to make slaked lime.

questions.question_text: Which elements are present in baking powder (NaHCO₃)?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Baking powder (sodium bicarbonate) NaHCO₃ contains sodium, hydrogen, carbon, and oxygen - four elements total.

questions.question_text: What is the molar mass of HCl? (Atomic masses: H = 1u, Cl = 35.5u)
questions.subject: science

questions.question_text: What is the mass of 1 mole of nitrogen atoms? (Atomic mass N = 14u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 14g - The mass of 1 mole of any element in grams equals its atomic mass in u.

questions.question_text: What is the mass of 4 moles of aluminium atoms? (Atomic mass Al = 27u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 108g - Mass = moles × molar mass = 4 × 27g = 108g

questions.question_text: How many moles are in 12g of oxygen gas (O₂)? (Atomic mass O = 16u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 0.375 moles - Molar mass of O₂ = 32g. Moles = 12g ÷ 32g = 0.375

questions.question_text: How many moles are in 22g of carbon dioxide (CO₂)? (Atomic masses: C = 12u, O = 16u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 0.5 moles - Molar mass of CO₂ = 44g. Moles = 22g ÷ 44g = 0.5

questions.question_text: What is the mass of 0.2 moles of oxygen atoms? (Atomic mass O = 16u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 3.2g - Mass = moles × molar mass = 0.2 × 16g = 3.2g

questions.question_text: What is the mass of 0.5 moles of water molecules? (Molecular mass H₂O = 18u)
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: 9g - Mass = moles × molar mass = 0.5 × 18g = 9g

questions.question_text: Which has more inertia: a rubber ball or a stone of the same size?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Stone has more inertia because inertia depends on mass. For the same size, stone has greater mass than rubber, so it has more inertia (resistance to change in motion).

questions.question_text: Which has more inertia: a bicycle or a train?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Train has much more inertia because of its enormous mass compared to a bicycle. Greater mass means greater resistance to change in motion.

questions.question_text: Inertia of an object depends on its:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Inertia depends only on mass. It's the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. Greater mass = greater inertia.

questions.question_text: Why do leaves fall off when we vigorously shake a tree branch?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: When the branch moves, inertia of the leaves resists this motion. Weakly attached leaves cannot overcome this resistance and fall off, while firmly attached ones stay.

questions.question_text: Why do you fall forward when a moving bus suddenly stops?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: When the bus stops, your body tends to continue moving forward due to inertia of motion. The lower part (touching the bus) stops, but the upper part continues forward.

questions.question_text: Why do you fall backward when a bus starts moving from rest?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: When the bus accelerates forward, your body tends to remain at rest due to inertia of rest. The lower part moves with the bus, but upper part stays back, making you fall backward.

questions.question_text: How can a horse pull a cart if action and reaction are equal and opposite?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Horse pushes ground backward; ground pushes horse forward (reaction). This forward force on horse moves both horse and cart. Action-reaction act on different objects, not canceling each other.

questions.question_text: Why is it difficult for a fireman to hold a hose ejecting water at high velocity?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: As water is ejected forward, an equal reaction force pushes the hose (and fireman) backward (Newton's 3rd law), making it hard to hold steady.

questions.question_text: When a carpet is beaten with a stick, why does dust come out?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: The stick moves the carpet forward, but dust particles tend to remain at rest due to inertia. This relative motion separates dust from carpet, making it come out.

questions.question_text: Why should luggage on a bus roof be tied with a rope?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: When the bus accelerates or brakes suddenly, inertia causes luggage to move in opposite direction. Tying prevents it from falling off due to these inertial forces.

questions.question_text: A cricket ball rolling on level ground slows down and stops. Why?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Friction between the ball and ground opposes the motion, gradually slowing it down until it stops. Without friction (like on ice), it would roll much farther.

questions.question_text: What is momentum?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Momentum = mass × velocity. It's the quantity of motion in a body. Heavier or faster objects have more momentum and are harder to stop.

questions.question_text: According to Newton's first law of motion, an object will continue in its state of rest or uniform motion unless:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Newton's 1st law states that an unbalanced external force is required to change an object's state of motion. Without such force, objects maintain their state.

questions.question_text: Newton's second law of motion can be expressed as:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Newton's 2nd law: Force = mass × acceleration (F = ma). Greater force causes greater acceleration; greater mass requires more force for same acceleration.

questions.question_text: Newton's third law of motion states:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: For every action, there's an equal and opposite reaction. These forces act on different objects. Example: You push wall; wall pushes you back equally.

questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of momentum?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Momentum = mass × velocity. SI unit = kg × m/s = kg·m/s (kilogram meter per second).

questions.question_text: The law of conservation of momentum states that:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: In an isolated system (no external forces), total momentum before collision equals total momentum after collision. Momentum is transferred but total remains constant.

questions.question_text: If two objects collide and stick together, what happens to their total momentum?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Total momentum is conserved. Before collision: m₁v₁ + m₂v₂. After collision: (m₁+m₂)v. Both are equal by conservation of momentum.

questions.question_text: A truck and a car are moving with the same velocity. Which has more momentum?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Momentum = mass × velocity. Since truck has much greater mass than car (same velocity), truck has more momentum.

questions.question_text: When you push a wall, it doesn't move. According to Newton's third law, does the wall push back?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: True - The wall pushes back with equal force. The wall doesn't move because it's massive and friction with ground is high, but the reaction force exists.

questions.question_text: If a moving cabinet requires 200 N force to move at constant velocity, what is the friction force?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Constant velocity means zero acceleration, so net force is zero. Applied force (200 N) must equal friction force. Friction = -200 N (opposite direction).

questions.question_text: Two objects of equal mass move in opposite directions with equal speed and collide, sticking together. What is their velocity after collision?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Before collision: m(+v) + m(-v) = 0. By conservation of momentum, after collision: 2m(v_final) = 0. Therefore v_final = 0. They come to rest.

questions.question_text: Why doesn't a massive parked truck move when you push it with small force?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: High static friction between truck and road equals and opposes your applied force. Net force is zero, so truck doesn't accelerate (F_net = ma, if F_net = 0, then a = 0).

questions.question_text: What is the relationship between force and momentum?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Force = rate of change of momentum = (change in momentum) / time. This is Newton's 2nd law in terms of momentum: F = Δp/Δt = m(v-u)/t = ma.

questions.question_text: Two objects of different masses falling freely near the surface of the moon would:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: All objects in free fall experience the same acceleration due to gravity (g), regardless of mass. Therefore, they have the same velocities at any instant.

questions.question_text: Where is the value of acceleration due to gravity (g) least?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: g is least at the equator because Earth bulges there - distance from center is maximum. g = GM/R², so larger R means smaller g.

questions.question_text: If masses of both objects are halved (without changing distance), the gravitational force becomes:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: F = Gm₁m₂/r². If both masses halved: F_new = G(m₁/2)(m₂/2)/r² = Gm₁m₂/4r² = F/4. Force becomes one-fourth.

questions.question_text: A boy whirls a stone in a horizontal circle. If the string breaks, the stone will:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: By Newton's 1st law, the stone moves in a straight line tangent to the circle. Centripetal force (string tension) kept it in circular path; without it, inertia takes over.

questions.question_text: An object floats with more volume outside liquid in d₁, less in d₃. Which is correct about densities?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: More volume outside means liquid is denser (supports object better). Less volume outside means liquid is less dense. Therefore: d₁ < d₂ < d₃.

questions.question_text: In the relation F = GMm/d², the quantity G is:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: G is the universal gravitational constant (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ Nm²/kg²), same everywhere in the universe. It doesn't depend on any specific body or location.

questions.question_text: Law of gravitation gives the gravitational force between:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Newton's law of gravitation is universal - it applies to ANY two bodies with mass, from atoms to galaxies. F = Gm₁m₂/r².

questions.question_text: The value of G in the law of gravitation:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: G is a universal constant (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ Nm²/kg²), independent of any planet's mass or radius. It's the same everywhere in the universe.

questions.question_text: If mass of each particle is doubled (distance unchanged), gravitational force becomes:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: F = Gm₁m₂/r². If both doubled: F_new = G(2m₁)(2m₂)/r² = 4Gm₁m₂/r² = 4F. Force becomes 4 times.

questions.question_text: What holds the atmosphere to the Earth?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Earth's gravity pulls atmospheric gases toward the planet, preventing them from escaping into space. Without gravity, atmosphere would disperse.

questions.question_text: The force between two unit masses separated by unit distance is called:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: When m₁ = m₂ = r = 1 in F = Gm₁m₂/r², we get F = G. This defines G - the universal gravitational constant.

questions.question_text: What is the weight of an object at the center of the Earth?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: At Earth's center, g = 0 (gravitational forces from all directions cancel out). Since Weight = mg, and g = 0, weight = 0.

questions.question_text: An object weighs 10 N in air and 8 N when fully immersed in water. What is the buoyant force?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Buoyant force = Weight in air - Weight in liquid = 10 N - 8 N = 2 N. This equals the weight of water displaced by the object.

questions.question_text: A box has dimensions 60×40×20 cm. Pressure is maximum when which face forms the base?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Pressure = Force/Area. For same force (weight), smaller area gives higher pressure. 40×20 = 800 cm² is smallest area, so maximum pressure.

questions.question_text: Apple falls from tree. If F₁ is force on apple by Earth and F₂ is force on Earth by apple, then:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: By Newton's 3rd law, for every action there's equal and opposite reaction. F₁ = F₂, but Earth's huge mass means negligible acceleration.

questions.question_text: What provides the centripetal force for a planet revolving around the Sun?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Gravitational attraction between Sun and planet provides the centripetal force needed for circular/elliptical orbit. Without it, planet would move in straight line.

questions.question_text: A stone is thrown horizontally and another is dropped from same height simultaneously. Which reaches ground first?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Both reach simultaneously. Vertical motion is independent of horizontal motion. Both have same vertical acceleration (g) and same initial vertical velocity (0).

questions.question_text: If Earth's gravity suddenly becomes zero, the Moon would:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Without gravitational centripetal force, Moon would move in straight line tangent to its orbit (Newton's 1st law - inertia).

questions.question_text: Packets dropped from same height at equator and pole. Which takes longer to reach ground?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: g is less at equator (Earth bulges there), so acceleration is less. Lower acceleration means longer time: t = √(2h/g). Equator packet falls slower.

questions.question_text: Weight on Moon is 1/6 of Earth. A person can lift 15 kg on Earth. Maximum mass liftable on Moon?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Same force on Moon: F = 15g_earth. On Moon: F = m × g_moon = m × (g_earth/6). Therefore: m = 15 × 6 = 90 kg.

questions.question_text: Why doesn't Earth fall into the Sun despite Sun's gravitational pull?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Sun's gravity provides centripetal force for Earth's orbit. Earth's orbital motion creates centrifugal effect. These balance, keeping Earth in stable orbit.

questions.question_text: Two bricks tied together will fall faster than one brick under gravity. Is this correct?
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: False - All objects fall with same acceleration (g) regardless of mass. Two bricks fall at same rate as one brick when air resistance is negligible.

questions.question_text: Buoyant force experienced by an object depends on:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Buoyant force = Volume of displaced liquid × Density of liquid × g. It depends on both volume displaced and liquid density.

questions.question_text: A cube immersed in saturated salt solution experiences greater buoyant force than in water because:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Buoyant force = Volume × Density of liquid × g. Salt solution has higher density than water, so greater buoyant force for same volume.

questions.question_text: If a cube's sides are reduced from 5 cm to 4 cm, buoyant force will:
questions.subject: science
questions.answer_explanation: Buoyant force ∝ Volume. Volume reduces from 125 cm³ (5³) to 64 cm³ (4³). Less volume displaced means less buoyant force.
